---
title: "Frozen Encoder + Hidden Activations — Plain Explanation"
---

This chapter unpacks two terms that appear in the [Alibi Detect AiSmartOps rundown](alibi_detect_aismartops_rundown.qmd), because they are really one combined idea: “use someone else’s already-trained vision system as a fixed feature extractor.”

## 1. What an “encoder” is

A neural network for image classification has two conceptual halves:

```text
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                              │
 │ Input image                 ENCODER                HEAD      │
 │ (high-dim)                  (compresses)           (decides) │
 │                                                              │
 │ ┌──────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐│
 │ │          │        │ conv → conv →   │      │ Dense layer  ││
 │ │ 224x224  │───────►│ pool → conv →   │─────►│ → softmax    ││
 │ │ x3 RGB   │        │ ... → flatten   │      │ → probs      ││
 │ │ ~150K    │        │                 │      └──────────────┘│
 │ │ numbers  │        │ outputs:        │              │       │
 │ └──────────┘        │ 2048-dim vector │              ▼       │
 │                     └─────────────────┘            "cat"     │
 │                              ▲                               │
 │                              │                               │
 │                     This vector is the                       │
 │                     "feature embedding"                      │
 │                     or "hidden activation"                   │
 │                                                              │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The encoder is the front part — many convolutional/transformer layers that progressively turn ~150,000 raw pixel numbers into a compact ~2,000-number vector. That vector is a summary of the image: “high contrast edges here, lung-like texture there, rounded opacity in upper-right region…” — except it’s the network’s own learned numeric language, not human words.

The head is the back part — usually a small dense layer that maps the summary vector to class probabilities.

## 2. What “hidden activation” means

In a neural network, every layer produces a tensor of numbers when you push an input through. Those numbers are called activations because they represent how strongly each neuron “fires” for that input.

```text
 Input ──► Layer 1 ──► Layer 2 ──► Layer 3 ──► ... ──► Output
              │           │           │
              ▼           ▼           ▼
           activ_1     activ_2     activ_3
          (visible)   ("hidden")  ("hidden")
```

The output layer’s activation is the visible prediction (e.g., class probabilities). All the intermediate layers’ activations are called hidden — not because they’re secret, just because they’re internal to the network and not normally exposed.

When we say “hidden activation of layer −2” (the second-to-last layer, just before the classification head), we mean the rich feature vector that contains everything the network has learned about the image, before it gets squashed down to a class label.

This is the part we want for drift detection — it’s the network’s “understanding” of the image.

## 3. What “frozen” means

A neural network has two states it can be in:

```text
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                                 │
 │ TRAINABLE (default)                  FROZEN (locked)            │
 │ ─────────────────                    ──────────────             │
 │                                                                 │
 │ Weights update with                  Weights NEVER change.      │
 │ every training step.                 Same input → same output,  │
 │ Same input today vs.                 forever.                   │
 │ next week → different                Pure deterministic         │
 │ output.                              function.                  │
 │                                                                 │
 │ for param in model.parameters():                                │
 │     param.requires_grad = True    ◄── default                   │
 │                                                                 │
 │ for param in model.parameters():                                │
 │     param.requires_grad = False   ◄── frozen                    │
 │                                                                 │
 │ model.eval()  ← also disables dropout, batch-norm updates       │
 │                                                                 │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

In PyTorch, freezing is literally setting `requires_grad = False` on every weight. The model still runs forward (inference still works), but no gradient is computed and no weight updates happen. The network becomes a fixed mathematical function — image in, vector out, identical every time.

## 4. Why both pieces matter together for drift detection

Here’s the radiology-flavored reasoning:

```text
 Why "encoder"?      → We need to compare images, but raw pixels are
                       too high-dimensional and too noisy. The encoder
                       compresses each image into a meaningful 2048-dim
                       summary where statistical comparison is feasible.

 Why "pretrained"?   → Training a new encoder from scratch on Ramathibodi
                       data would itself be a model, with its own drift,
                       its own bias, its own validation burden. We want
                       to BORROW someone else's already-trained vision
                       system — RadImageNet, DINOv2, MedSAM's encoder —
                       and use it as a reference measuring stick.

 Why "frozen"?       → If the encoder's weights changed, the same chest
                       X-ray would map to different points in feature
                       space at different times. Drift detection would
                       become meaningless — we couldn't tell if drift
                       comes from the data or from the measuring stick.
                       Freezing guarantees the measuring stick is
                       constant.
```

A radiology analogy: imagine you want to track whether the patients arriving at your hospital this month “look different” from last year’s cohort. You hire a senior radiologist who has trained for 30 years on diverse imaging from many institutions, and you ask them to summarize each new study with a fixed checklist of 2,048 visual descriptors — same checklist every time, for every patient, forever. They are frozen (no new learning) and they are an encoder (they compress a complex image into a fixed-length summary). Over time, you compare summary distributions. That is what `preprocess_drift(model=HiddenOutput(model, layer=-2))` does in code.

## 5. The full picture in one diagram

```text
 Reference cohort                         Today's cohort
 (validation set,                         (last 7 days of
 what was "normal" at PIE)                production studies)

 ┌───────────────┐                        ┌───────────────┐
 │ 1000 chest    │                        │ 200 chest     │
 │ X-rays        │                        │ X-rays        │
 └───────┬───────┘                        └───────┬───────┘
         │                                        │
         │          Frozen encoder                │
         │          (RadImageNet ResNet-50,       │
         │          requires_grad = False,        │
         │          .eval() mode)                 │
         ▼                                        ▼
 ┌───────────────┐                        ┌───────────────┐
 │ 1000 vectors  │                        │ 200 vectors   │
 │ × 2048 dim    │                        │ × 2048 dim    │
 │ "ref features"│                        │ "cur features"│
 └───────┬───────┘                        └───────┬───────┘
         │                                        │
         └──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                        │
                        ▼
              ┌────────────────────┐
              │ MMD test:          │
              │ are these two      │
              │ point clouds       │
              │ from the same      │
              │ distribution?      │
              │                    │
              │ p < 0.05 ⇒         │
              │ DRIFT DETECTED     │
              └────────────────────┘
```

So when the [Alibi Detect chapter](alibi_detect_aismartops_rundown.qmd) says “hidden activations of a frozen encoder,” it means: take a pretrained vision network, lock its weights so it never changes, push your medical images through it, and grab the internal feature vector from a layer just before its classification head. Those vectors become your numeric stand-in for the images, and that is what drift detection actually compares.
